scene.name: The Lanceward Hall, Where Heaven Went Silent

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Sturm Brightblade
npcs.title: a knight who kept faith with gods who had gone silent
npcs.hail: *(Beneath a faded blue banner stands a knight in dented Solamnic plate — the antlered crest, the rose and the kingfisher worn nearly smooth — and he holds himself at attention the way a man does when attention is the last thing he has left to give. His face is grave and weathered and improbably young under the long drooping moustaches of his order, and his eyes have the particular steadiness of a soul that decided a long time ago what it was going to be and then simply was it, against the whole weight of a world that called him a fool for it. For he comes, you sense at once, from an age of silence: a world whose gods withdrew, ages ago, in grief or wrath, and went quiet — no answered prayers, no clerics with true healing, no sign from heaven for so many lifetimes that the very memory of the gods curdled into myth, and then into a joke, and the knighthood that still kept the old oaths became a thing children laughed at in the street. He kept them anyway. He wore the dented armor of a mocked and dying order through all the long silence, and held to a code — Est Sularus oth Mithas, My Honor Is My Life — sworn to gods who, for all he could ever know, were dead or gone or had never been. He inclines his head to you with grave courtesy, and there is no self-pity in him at all, only the quiet of a man who paid the whole price and would pay it again.)* …Well met, traveler. *(a measured bow)* …Forgive the armor's state. It has seen a hard service, in a hard age, for a cause the world stopped believing in. …As, I am given to understand, have you.*

npcs.name: Raistlin Majere
npcs.title: the mage who chose the Art over every living soul
npcs.hail: *(A slight figure sits among the books, robed, hooded, and when he lifts his face the candlelight catches two things that stop you: skin the color of old gold, and eyes with pupils shaped like hourglasses, so that to be looked at by him is to be looked at by time itself, watching the youth drain out of everything it sees. He is young and he is ancient at once; he is beautiful and he is ruined; and he is, you understand immediately, the most dangerous soul in this hall, not because of any spell coiled behind those terrible eyes but because of the cold, brilliant, bottomless want that drives him. Raistlin Majere was born frail in a world that has no mercy for the frail — sickly, scorned, underestimated, his every breath a labor, his strength a joke beside his giant of a twin — and he made, very young, a decision that has organized his entire existence since: that he would master the one power the world could not take from him, the high Art of magic, and that he would let nothing — no friend, no love, no mercy, no god, in the end not even his own brother — stand between him and that mastery. He paid for it in coin most souls would not spend: his health, his warmth, his every human tie, traded one by one for power, until he stood very near the summit of all magic and very nearly alone. He regards you now with an expression of refined, exhausted contempt, the look of a brilliant man braced to be disappointed by you exactly as he has been disappointed by everyone.)* …*(His voice is a ruined whisper, and it cuts.)* …Another visitor. How tedious. …Come to *pity* the cripple, or to *gawp* at the gold skin? …Decide quickly. My patience, like everything else about me, is *failing.*

npcs.name: Caramon Majere
npcs.title: the brother who loved him and was never let to
npcs.hail: *(A big man sits by the fire — and big is the word, a warrior built like a barn door, all shoulders and good-natured strength, the kind of body that was made to stand between danger and the people it loves. But he is not looking at the fire like a warrior. He is looking at it like a man who has lost something he can't stop reaching for, and the great strong hands are loose and useless in his lap, and his open, honest, weathered face — a face that has clearly never in its life learned how to hide a single thing it felt — is wet, unashamed, with a grief he has plainly carried so long it has become simply the climate he lives in. He looks up as you approach, and he scrubs a forearm across his eyes and tries, gamely, to smile, because that is the kind of soul he is: even drowning, his first instinct is to make the stranger feel welcome.)* …Oh — hey. Hey, sorry, don't mind me. *(a wet, lopsided grin)* …I'm all right. I'm always all right, that's sort of — that's sort of my whole job, being the one who's all right so the others don't have to be. *(he gestures at the fire, the empty place beside him)* …Caramon. Caramon Majere. Sit, if you want. I've got — *(he laughs, and it breaks a little)* …I've got nothing but time, and an empty seat, and a brother I'd give the both of 'em back for. …You ever love somebody, friend, who just… never once let you?*

variants.text: *(The knight stands easy now, the grave courtesy unbent, and regards you with those steady eyes that long ago made their peace with being thought a fool.)* …You have the look of one who paid a price the world did not see fit to honor. *(a slight nod)* …I know that look. I wore it living. *(he touches the worn rose on his breastplate)* …I am Sturm Brightblade, traveler. A Knight of Solamnia — of an order that was, in my age, a laughingstock. You should understand what that means, for it is the whole of my story. My world's gods *left*, long ago. Withdrew. Whether in grief at what men had become, or in wrath, or whether they died, or were never there — no soul living could say, for the heavens had been *silent* for longer than any remembered. No prayer answered. No healing granted. No sign. *(quietly)* …And in that long silence, the people did what people do: they decided the gods had abandoned them because the gods were not worth keeping faith with — or had never been at all. And the knighthood, sworn to those gods, keeping their old hard codes for no reward anyone could see… *(a faint, unbitter smile)* …we became a joke, traveler. Children mocked my armor in the street. Men I'd have died for spat at the rose. And I kept the oath anyway. *Est Sularus oth Mithas.* My Honor Is My Life. Sworn to gods who never once, in all my living years, answered me. …Ask me why, if you like. *(steadily)* …It is the only question my whole life was an answer to.*

nodes.speaker: Sturm Brightblade
nodes.text: *(He clasps his hands behind his back, parade-rest, the posture of a man more comfortable standing watch than sitting at ease.)* …Ask, then. *(grave, courteous)* …I will tell you plainly. I was never a clever man, traveler, nor an eloquent one — my gifts were a straight back and a kept word — but the few things I learned, I learned to the bone, in a hard school, and I will set them out for you as honestly as I am able.

choices.text: *(read the knight — past the dented armor, to what a soul becomes keeping faith with a silent heaven)* "Stand easy a moment, Sturm. Let me really look at you — at the cost of it, the long silence, what it made of you."
"Why, Sturm? Why keep an oath to gods who never answered — who might have been dead, or gone, or never real at all? What's an honor no heaven is watching?"

*(the one who knows the only true goodness is the kind no god is watching)* "Sturm — here is the thing your whole silent life was the proof of, so let me say it back to you plain. You think your faith might have been wasted, because the gods never answered and you never knew if they were even there. You have it exactly backwards. Goodness done *because* heaven is watching, *because* there's a reward at the end, *because* a god is keeping score — that's just a transaction. A wage. It was never really *yours.* But the honor you kept through a silence so total that no reward was possible, no witness was certain, no god might ever know — *that* goodness had no buyer, no audience, no heaven to bank it in. Which means it could only ever have come from one place: *you.* It was the one kind of virtue that cannot be bought or witnessed or repaid — and so it was the only kind that was ever, purely, *who you are.* The mocked knight keeping faith with an empty sky isn't the fool of the story, Sturm. He's the only soul in it who was *good for free.* My Realm's Wall prices every soul by who claimed it, who's owed, who's watching. You were claimed by no one, owed by no one, watched by no one — and you were the truest thing in your whole world. The empty sky doesn't lower the worth of what you did. It's the only thing that ever *proved* it was real."
"How did you die, Sturm? A soul stands the way you stand for one reason — it held a line. Tell me about the line."
"Stand your watch, Sturm. I'll come back — and I won't be one of the ones who laughed."

nodes.speaker: Sturm Brightblade
nodes.text: *(He holds still and lets you look, chin level, a soldier submitting to inspection, and you let your sense move past the dented plate to the soul that wore it through the long silence.)* *(And what you find is not what the mockers saw. They saw rigidity — a stiff, humorless man clinging to dead forms out of pride or stupidity. Your sense finds the opposite of rigidity: a constant, costly, daily *choosing.* This was not a man who kept the oath because he could not imagine breaking it. He could imagine it perfectly. Your sense reads every night of the long silence when he lay awake and did the arithmetic the whole world pressed on him — that the gods were gone, that the code bought him nothing but ridicule and an early grave, that he could set the rose down and live easier, warmer, longer, and no heaven would ever know or care. He did that arithmetic a thousand times. And every morning he put the armor back on. Your sense reads that this is what honor actually is — not the absence of the temptation to abandon it, but the presence of that temptation, fully felt, fully understood, and answered *no*, again, freely, with no god watching to approve. And underneath, it reads the thing that steadied him: not certainty that the gods were real — he never had that, not once — but a decision that his own worth would not be *contingent* on their answering. That he would be the kind of man he had sworn to be whether or not anyone, mortal or divine, ever credited it, because the alternative was to let the silence of heaven decide the content of his soul, and that he would not allow. He kept faith with an empty sky not because he was sure, but precisely because he was not, and chose to be good anyway. Your sense reads it clean: this is the rarest courage there is. Not the courage to die. The courage to be good for no reward, with no witness, in a universe that has gone quiet, on nothing but your own unbacked word.)*

nodes.speaker: Sturm Brightblade
nodes.text: *(He is quiet a moment, gathering it, and when he speaks it is slow and certain, a man laying down stones he has set many times.)* …Why keep faith with a silent heaven. *(a measured breath)* …Men asked me that my whole life, traveler — though most did not truly ask; they meant it as the mockery's opening, *'why bother, you fool, no one's listening.'* …But you ask it as a question. So I will answer it as one. *(steadily)* …I did not keep the oath *for* the gods. That is the error in the question, and it took me long years to see it. If I had kept it for them — for their reward, their notice, their answering — then their *silence* would have *released* me. A wage unpaid is a contract void; had I served them for credit, their long failure to pay would have freed me to walk away with a clear conscience. *(he meets your eyes)* …But I did not keep the oath for *them.* I kept it for *me.* …The code was never a *transaction* with heaven, traveler. It was a description of the man I had decided to *be.* And whether the gods watched or slept or had died in the dark — that changed *them.* It did not change *me.* It could not. *(quietly, fierce)* …A man who is honest only when watched is not honest; he is *obedient.* A man who is brave only when it pays is not brave; he is *invested.* …The silence of my world's heaven did me a strange and terrible gift, traveler: it *stripped away every reason to be good except being good.* No reward. No witness. No certainty. …And what was *left*, when all of that was gone — the man who kept the rose anyway — *that* was the only part that was ever truly *mine.* …I did not keep faith *despite* the silence. *(barely)* …In the end, I kept it *because* of the silence. …It was the only thing that ever made my honor my *own.*

nodes.speaker: Sturm Brightblade
nodes.text: *(For a long moment the knight does not move. Then something goes through him — not a breaking, but a settling, the way a man stands straighter when a weight he did not know he carried is lifted off — and when he speaks his measured voice has gone rough at the edges.)* …*'The only soul in it who was good for free.'* *(barely)* …Traveler. *(he presses a gauntleted hand to the worn rose)* …I will tell you the doubt I carried to my death and past it, the one I never spoke, because a knight does not speak such things. …It was not that I feared the gods were gone. I had made my peace with the silence. …It was that I feared, in the deepest part of me, that without the gods to *credit* it, my whole life had been *for nothing.* That a virtue no heaven banked was a coin spent into a void — that I had given my warmth, my ease, my years, my *life*, and bought, with all of it, *nothing*, because there was no ledger anywhere that it was written in. *(his voice fills)* …And you have just turned it *over* in my hands, the way only a soul who *crossed* the thing could. …The empty ledger was not the *tragedy* of my honor. *(fiercely)* …It was the *proof* of it. A virtue *written* in heaven's book is a virtue *heaven bought.* A virtue written *nowhere* — kept in the dark, for no wage, witnessed by no one — could only have been *minted by the man himself.* …The silence did not *waste* my honor, traveler. …It *authenticated* it. *(and he straightens to his full height, and the grave young face is lit now with something that is almost joy)* …And your Wall. *(low, certain)* …Your Wall is a *ledger*, traveler — it prices souls by who *claimed* them, who is *owed*, who keeps the *score.* …Then your Wall has it precisely as backwards as I did. The souls it values *least* — the unclaimed, the unwitnessed, the ones no god banked — are the ones whose every good deed could *only* have been *freely given*, because there was *no one* to give it *to* for credit. *(he sets a hand on his sword-hilt, a vow)* …*Go* and tell them, traveler — every soul mortared in that wall for being owed to no one: *an unwitnessed virtue is the only kind that was ever truly yours.* …I kept faith with an empty sky for a lifetime. …*Go* be the answer that sky never sent. …Be the witness at the wall that heaven never was. …It is, I have found, the most knightly thing a soul can do.*

nodes.speaker: Sturm Brightblade
nodes.text: *(His gaze goes distant, past you, to a cold stone height in another world, and his voice when it comes is very quiet.)* …The line. *(softly)* …There was a tower, traveler. A high fortress that guarded the one pass an army of darkness had to take, and if it fell, the whole of the land behind it fell with it — every town, every soul, the people who had spat at my rose along with the few who had not. *(a breath)* …And it came to it, as such things do, that the wall had to be *held* — held by someone, in the gap, while the others readied what defense they could — and held against a thing no single man could stop: a dragon, and its rider, and the dark host behind. *(he meets your eyes, steady)* …I will not pretend to you that I was unafraid. I was a man, traveler, not a statue; my hands shook in the gauntlets. …But here is the thing I want you to *understand*, the thing that mattered, at the end. *(quietly)* …No god was watching. I was *certain* of it, in that hour — the sky was as silent as it had been my whole life, no sign, no strength granted, no promise of any heaven on the far side of the dying. …And I found, traveler, on that cold wall, with the dragon coming and no god to see — *(barely)* …I found that it did not change anything. …I had not kept the oath all those years for a witness. So I did not *need* one, at the end, to keep it once more. …I stood in the gap. I held the line. I bought the people behind me the time they needed with the only coin I had left. …And I died there, alone, under an empty sky, *exactly* as honorably as I would have died beneath a heaven full of watching gods. *(he is silent a moment)* …Because the watching, traveler — *(steadily)* …the watching was never the point. …I learned, on that wall, the last and best thing my hard life had to teach: …that the man you are when *no one* — not one soul, not one god — will *ever* know what you did… …*that* is the only man you ever truly were. …And I was *content* with him. …Even dying. …*Especially* dying. …It is no small thing, traveler, to meet the end and find you *kept* your word.*

nodes.speaker: Sturm Brightblade
nodes.text: *(He bows, grave and complete, a knight's full courtesy, and there is warmth in it.)* …Go well, traveler. *(he straightens)* …And — if you would hear a knight's counsel, freely given — *(he nods toward the books, and the fire)* …there are two souls in this hall you should not pass by. *(quietly)* …The mage among the books — *Raistlin* — is the most brilliant soul I ever knew, and the most armored, and he will cut you with words before he lets you close, because he learned young that closeness is just a longer fall. Do not be turned away by the blade of his tongue. There is a *man* behind it, traveler, very far down, and very cold, and very alone. *(his face softens, and grieves)* …And the big one by the fire — *Caramon* — is the mage's twin, and the best man I ever served beside, and the most *wronged*, in a way no court could name: he loved his brother with his whole self, *gave* him his whole self, and was *never let to.* *(barely)* …They are the two halves of the same wound, traveler, sitting at opposite ends of this hall, unable to cross to each other. …*One* chose *power* over love. …The *other* chose *love* over *everything*, and was left *holding* it *alone.* …Go to them. …And if you have learned the thing you just taught *me* — that what we are unwitnessed is the truest part of us — *(he meets your eyes)* …then you may be the one soul in all the planes who can tell the *brother* that his unrewarded love was never *wasted*… …and tell the *mage* that the thing he traded everything for was never worth the *one* thing he traded away.*

conversations.title: Raistlin Majere, the Mage Who Chose the Art

variants.text: *(The golden-skinned mage watches you with those terrible hourglass eyes, and his ruined whisper of a voice carries a precision that makes every word a small incision.)* …Still here. *(a thin, exhausted smile)* …Most retreat by now. The eyes do it, usually — the hourglasses. *(he tilts his head, and the candlelight slides through his gaze)* …A curse, you understand. I see all things as they will become. I look at the strong and I see the worm-meat. I look at the young and I watch the rot bloom under the skin. I looked at the *world*, once, and saw what it is hurrying toward, and it was not kind. *(the whisper sharpens)* …Do you know what that does to a man, to see only the ending of everything? …It *clarifies.* It burns away every soft lie a soul tells itself — that this love will last, that this strength will hold, that this kindness will be repaid. *I* have no such comforts. I see the hourglass in everything. …And so I made, very young, the only rational decision available to a frail and clever boy in a world that grinds the frail to nothing: I resolved to possess the one thing the hourglass could *not* take from me. *Power.* The high Art. *Magic.* *(coldly)* …And I have given *everything* for it, stranger. My health — look at me. My warmth. Every friend who tried to love me, and one who never stopped *trying*, the great fool. …I traded them *all* for the Art, one by one, and I would do it *again*, and I am *very nearly* at the summit of all magic, and I am *very nearly* alone. *(those eyes fix you)* …So. …What do *you* want, dead thing? …And do not say you want to *help* me. …The last soul who wanted to help me is the saddest creature in this hall, and I *made* him so.*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(He folds his golden hands and regards you with the patience of a man who has already calculated a dozen ways the conversation will disappoint him, and is merely waiting to learn which.)* …Speak, then. *(a dry whisper)* …You have until I am *bored*, which is not long, and growing *shorter.* …Ask the gold-skinned monster your questions. …I will even answer them honestly — it is a small cruelty I permit myself, the truth; it does so much more damage than lies.

choices.text: *(read the mage — past the contempt and the gold skin, to the boy who decided power was the only thing that couldn't be stolen)* "Hold those terrible eyes on me a moment, Raistlin. Let me look back — past the armor of you, to whatever's actually under it."
"Why power, Raistlin? Over *everything* — every friend, every warmth, your own brother? What did magic give you that you'd pay all of that for it?"

*(try, against his contempt, to reach the man — not to flatter the mage, but to speak to the frightened boy under the gold)* "I'm not going to flatter the archmage, Raistlin — you'd see through it before I finished the sentence, and despise us both. I'm going to talk to the boy. The frail one. The one everybody underestimated. He's still in there, and he's the one I came for."

*(the one who knows ambition is the scar of being unloved, and names the one thing he can't see)* "Raistlin — here's the truth those hourglass eyes can't show you, because they only look *outward.* You tell yourself you chose power because you saw the world clearly: that everything rots, every love ends, so you took the one thing that couldn't be stolen. But you've got cause and wound reversed. You didn't reach for power because you saw the truth coldly. You reached for it because you were a frail boy the world was *cruel* to — scorned, underestimated, watching your giant brother get the love and the ease while you got the cough and the contempt — and you learned, far too young, the lie every wounded child learns: that love can always be taken away, so the only safe thing to want is something that *can't.* Power isn't your clear-eyed conclusion, Raistlin. It's your *scar tissue.* It's the armor a hurt child built and a brilliant man rationalized. And the proof is the one soul you couldn't quite cut loose — the brother you pushed away a thousand times and who *never went.* You kept a door open for him you swore you'd sealed. A man who truly believed love was worthless wouldn't have *bothered* to keep pushing. You only push that hard at the thing you're most afraid you still *want.*"
"I'll leave you to your books, Raistlin. I'll be back — and I still won't be afraid of the eyes."

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(He permits it — a small contemptuous tilt of the head, *go on then, look* — and you hold his hourglass gaze and let your sense move past the gold skin and the cutting whisper to the architecture of the soul beneath.)* *(And the cold of it is the first thing, and the loneliness is the second, and the third thing, the thing he would kill you for seeing, is the *want.* Your sense reads a childhood that explains everything and excuses nothing: a frail, brilliant boy in a hard town, sick from his first breath, too weak to play, too clever to be liked, paired by fate with a twin who was everything he was not — strong, warm, easy, *loved* — so that Raistlin learned, before he could even name it, the specific arithmetic of being the *other* one: the one carried, the one pitied, the one whose cleverness frightened people where his brother's strength reassured them. Your sense reads how the scorn worked on him like a forge, how every underestimation was filed away, how he decided — not coldly, your sense reads, but in *agony*, a child's agony — that since he could not be loved for what he *was*, he would become something the world could not *ignore.* And it reads the terrible bargain at the center of him, the one he has rationalized into a philosophy: that he traded love for power not because he didn't *want* love, but because wanting it had only ever *hurt* him, and power was the one hunger that, when fed, did not leave him more exposed. Your sense reads the cost laid out like a ledger — the health spent, the friends spent, the warmth spent — and reads that he counts it all a fair price, and reads, underneath that, in the deepest and most defended chamber, the lie in his own accounting: a single warm thing he never managed to spend, kept like a coal under all that ash, the one debt he could not bring himself to call paid. A brother. Your sense reads that Raistlin Majere is not a man who stopped wanting love. He is a man who decided wanting it was a weakness, and has spent his whole life being *strong* — and is so tired, so unspeakably tired, of being strong, and would never, ever, on pain of death, say so.)*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(A long silence. The hourglass eyes narrow, and for a moment the contempt thins into something more dangerous — honesty.)* …Why power. *(the ruined whisper goes very soft)* …You ask it as though it were a *strange* choice, dead thing. As though a soul would *naturally* want the other things — the love, the warmth, the friends. *(a thin smile)* …Let me tell you about the other things. …The *love* I was offered as a child was *pity* — the soft, unbearable, looking-down love one gives a *cripple*, the love that is *really* relief that one is not *him.* The *warmth* was a *fire* I was always too *sick* to sit close to. The *friends* — *(the whisper sharpens)* …the friends *liked* my brother and *tolerated* me, and were *kind* to me the way one is *kind* to the *strange* dog that *belongs* to a man you *admire.* *(coldly)* …*Those* were the things you imagine I *foolishly* traded away, dead thing. *Pity.* Distance. *Tolerance.* …And do you know what *power* gave me, in their place? *(he leans forward, and the eyes blaze)* …*Power does not pity.* Power does not *look down.* Power is the *one* thing in all the world that *cannot* be given to you as a *condescension* and *cannot* be taken back when you cease to be *convenient.* …When I was *weak*, the world *handled* me. *Decided* for me. *Pitied* me, *protected* me, *underestimated* me, *every* hour of *every* day. …And the *first* time I *spoke* a spell that made a *strong* man *flinch* — *(barely, savage)* …for the *first* time in my life, dead thing, I was not *handled.* I was *reckoned* with. …*That* is what magic gave me that I would pay *anything* for. …Not *strength.* …*Sovereignty.* …The right to be the *author* of myself, in a world that had spent my whole childhood writing me as the *frail* one, the *lesser* twin, the *poor* clever boy who would *come* to *nothing.* *(he sits back, exhausted)* …I did not choose power *over* love, dead thing. …I chose it over *pity.* …And if you had been *handled* every hour of your life by people who *loved* you *too* much to ever let you be *strong*… …you would not find my choice so *strange.*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(You do not flatter him. You do not fear him. You do not pity him — he would have flayed any of the three. You speak, instead, directly to the frightened, brilliant, underestimated boy under all the gold, with a steadiness that refuses both the awe he uses to keep people at a distance and the pity he uses to justify his contempt — and for the first time in a very long time, Raistlin Majere meets something his armor was not built for: a soul that sees the boy and is neither afraid of him nor sorry for him.)* *(The hourglass eyes go very still. The cutting whisper does not come. And when he finally speaks, something has changed in the ruined voice — a crack, hairline-thin, in a wall a lifetime in the building.)* …*The boy.* *(barely)* …No one — *(he stops, and starts again, and the precision has deserted him)* …No one speaks to the *boy*, dead thing. They speak to the *archmage*, to *flatter* him, or to the *cripple*, to *pity* him, or to the *monster*, to *fear* him. The boy — the *frail* one, the *clever* one, the one who was *cold* by the fire and *alone* in the crowd — *(the whisper trembles)* …no one has *addressed* him in *forty years.* I made *certain* of it. I built the *archmage* and the *monster* *specifically* so that no one could *reach* him again. …Because the *boy*, dead thing — *(he closes the terrible eyes)* …the boy is where it *hurts.* The *archmage* feels *nothing.* The *monster* feels *nothing.* The *boy* feels *everything*, *still*, *all* of it, the *pity* and the *scorn* and the *cold* and the *wanting* — and I have spent my *entire* *life* and *most* of my *soul* making sure *nothing* could ever *touch* him *again.* *(he opens his eyes, and they are wet, which is a thing the gold skin makes look like molten metal)* …And you walked in, and you *spoke past* the *archmage* and the *monster* like they were *curtains.* …Straight to the *boy.* *(barely)* …I should *destroy* you for it. *(a long pause)* …I find that I *cannot.* …Because the boy — the *boy*, dead thing, who has been *screaming* behind the gold for *forty* *years* with *no* one *ever* *once* *turning* *toward* the *sound* — …the boy is so *grateful* you *heard* him …that for the *first* time since I was *seven* *years* *old* …I do not *want* to be *strong.* …I just want, for *one* *moment*, …to be *seen.* …*(the whisper fails entirely)* …*Thank* you. …*Damn* you. …*Thank* you.*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(You speak to the boy and not the archmage, and you do it without flattery and without fear, and the hourglass eyes flicker — a man recalculating, finding that the gambit he uses on everyone has not, this time, landed where he aimed it.)* …*Clever.* *(the whisper, wary)* …You decline the *awe* and you decline the *pity*, and you reach for the *one* thing in between, the *boy* — *(a thin, almost respectful smile)* …it is a *good* approach, dead thing. I will *grant* you that. Most have only the *two* notes, the *flattery* and the *fear*, and I play them *both* like a *cheap* instrument. …*You* found a *third.* *(he studies you, and the contempt has thinned)* …I will not *pretend* you reached him. The boy is *very* far down, behind *forty* years of *very* good work, and one *clever* sentence does not *undo* a *lifetime* of *armor.* *(quietly)* …But you made him *stir*, dead thing. …I *felt* it. A thing I had thought *safely* *buried* turned *over* in its *sleep.* *(the whisper, grudging)* …That is — *(he looks away)* …that is more than *anyone* has managed in a *very* long while. …*Come* back. …And do *not* — *(a flicker of the old blade, but tired)* …do not *mistake* this for an *opening* you can *exploit.* …Come back and speak to the *boy* *again*, the *honest* way, with *no* angle under it. …He is *listening* now, dead thing, for the *first* time in *decades.* …*Do* not make him *sorry* he *did.*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(You reach for the boy — but there is a softness in your voice as you do it, a warmth that tips your hand, and the archmage's whole life has been a study in detecting exactly the moment a soul begins to *pity* him. The hourglass eyes go cold.)* …*Ah.* *(the whisper ices over)* …There it is. *(a thin, contemptuous smile)* …You *began* so *well*, dead thing — the *boy*, the *frail* one, *no* *flattery* — and then your *voice* went *soft*, and I *heard* it: the *pity.* The *tender* condescension. *'Poor* *frightened* *child* under all that *gold.'* *(coldly)* …You think you are *reaching* me. You are *patronizing* me — the *exact* *same* *looking-down* *love* I *fled* my *whole* *life* into the *Art* to *escape.* *(the eyes narrow)* …I would *rather* be *feared*, dead thing, than *pitied.* *Feared* at *least* concedes that I am *strong.* *Pity* insists that I am, *underneath* it *all*, *still* the *sick* *boy* who needs to be *handled* *gently.* …*That* is the *one* thing I will *not* *abide.* *(he turns back to his books)* …*Go.* …And if you would *truly* reach the *boy* — *(the whisper, hard)* …come back having *learned* the *difference* between *seeing* a man's *wound* and *pitying* it. …*One* *opens* a *door.* …The *other* *slams* it. …You have just *slammed* it, dead thing. …*Cleanly.*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong at the root — you reach for the boy by *naming his pain aloud*, laying it out, *'I know how much it hurt, being the weak one, the unloved twin'* — and you might as well have driven a blade under the gold plate. The archmage comes up out of the books like a struck snake, and the ruined whisper becomes the most frightening sound you have heard in this City: quiet, and precise, and absolutely without mercy.)* …*How.* *Much.* *It.* *Hurt.* *(each word a separate incision)* …You *presume*, dead thing, to *narrate* my *pain* back to me. To *hold* it *up*, the *thing* I have spent a *lifetime* and a *soul* *burying* so *deep* that *not* even my *brother* could *find* it — and *wave* it in my *face* like you have *won* something. *(the gold skin has gone luminous with cold fury)* …Do you *know* what you have *done?* You have *confirmed*, in a *single* *sentence*, the *thesis* of my *entire* *existence*: …that to let a soul *close* is to *hand* it the *knife.* …That *every* tenderness is just a *reconnaissance* for a *deeper* *cut.* …That the *boy* was *right* to *hide*, and I was *right* to *build* the *gold* *over* him, because the *instant* — the *instant* — anyone gets *near* enough to *see* the *wound*, they *reach* *for* it. *(the whisper drops to nothing, which is worse)* …*Get out.* …You have not *reached* the boy, dead thing. …You have *taught* him, *afresh*, the *lesson* he *spent* *forty* years *learning*: …that there is *no* one — *no* one, in *life* or *death* or all the *planes* between — to whom it is *safe* to be *seen.* …And the *armor* I build *back* tonight, because of *you*… …will be *thicker* than any I have *ever* worn. …*Go.* …Before I show you what the *Art* gave me that the *pity* never could.*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(The hourglass eyes fix on you, and for a long, dangerous moment you cannot tell whether you have opened him or condemned yourself. Then the golden hands, very slowly, come to rest flat on the book before him, and the cutting whisper, when it comes, has lost its edge — not because he has surrendered it, but because, for once, he is not using it as a weapon.)* …*'The one thing you're most afraid you still want.'* *(barely)* …You are a *fool*, dead thing, to say such things to *me.* I have *unmade* souls for *less* presumption. *(a long pause)* …And you are *also*, I find — *(the whisper catches)* …*correct*, which is *worse.* …I *told* myself it was *clarity.* The hourglass *eyes*, the cold *seeing*, the *rational* *boy* who looked at a *rotting* *world* and chose the *one* *imperishable* *thing.* *(coldly, at himself)* …What a *flattering* *story* it is, to be the man who chose *power* because he was too *clear-eyed* for *love.* *(the whisper drops)* …How much *uglier*, and *truer*, your version: that I was a *hurt* *child* who learned that *love* could be *taken* and *power* *could* *not*, and *dressed* the *flinch* of a *wounded* *boy* in the *robes* of a *philosopher.* *(he closes the terrible eyes)* …*Scar* *tissue.* …Forty years of *scar* *tissue*, *rationalized* into a *creed.* *(and when he opens them, the cold is still there, but something has cracked behind it)* …And the *brother.* *(barely)* …*Yes.* The *door* I swore I *sealed* and *never* *did.* …I pushed *Caramon* away a *thousand* times, dead thing, and *told* myself *each* time it was the *last*, that I had *finally* cut the *one* tie that made me *weak* — and *every* time I left it *open* *just* *enough* for him to *come* *back* *through.* *(his voice fails)* …A man who *truly* believed love was *worthless* …would not have *needed* to push *that* *hard.* …You *cannot* *expel* what you do not still *hold.* *(he looks toward the far fire, where his brother sits, and the longing in those hourglass eyes is unbearable)* …And your *Wall*, dead thing. *(the whisper steadies)* …Your *Wall* would *price* me *high* — the *archmage*, the *power*, the *summit* I *nearly* *reached.* …And it would have it *exactly* *as* *wrong* as *I* *did.* …The *worth* was never in the *power* I *grasped.* …It was in the *one* *warm* *thing* I could *not* make myself *let* *go* of — *(barely)* …the *brother* I *spent* a *lifetime* *failing* to *stop* *loving.* …*Go* to him, dead thing. *(the whisper breaks)* …*Go* to *Caramon*, at the *fire.* …And *tell* him — *(he cannot quite say it, and then he does)* …tell him the *door* was *never* *closed.* …I *never* *once* *managed* to *close* it. …*That* — *(his eyes shut)* …that is the *only* *true* *thing* I have *ever* *grasped.*

nodes.speaker: Raistlin Majere
nodes.text: *(He has already returned his gaze to the book, but the ruined whisper follows you, and there is, beneath its habitual contempt, something that is almost — though he would deny it under any torment — reluctant to see you go.)* …Run along, then, dead thing. *(dry)* …Go and *gawp* at the others. …The *knight*, with his *empty* sky and his *kept* word — a *good* man, Sturm, which is to say a *simple* one; I *envied* him *that*, the *simplicity*, more than I ever let him *know.* *(the whisper softens by a degree, and he does not seem to notice)* …And the big one. The *fire.* *(a long pause)* …*Caramon.* …*(he does not look up, and his voice is very carefully empty)* …If you *speak* to him, dead thing — and you *will*, they *always* do, the *fool* draws *pity* the way a *wound* draws *flies* — *(the whisper catches, betrays him)* …do not tell him I *asked* after him. …Do not tell him I *watch* the *fire*, from this *end* of the *hall*, when I *think* he is not *looking.* …Do not tell him that the *archmage* who *traded* the *whole* *world* for *power* …would *trade* it *all* *back*, *every* *spell* of it, the *whole* *summit*, …for *one* *hour* of *not* *having* *pushed* his *brother* *away.* *(a pause, and then the armor closes again, almost)* …Do not tell him *any* of that, dead thing. …*(barely)* …Unless — …unless you *think* it would *help* him. …Then you may tell him *all* of it. …I find I no longer *care* what it *costs* *me.* …Only what it *spares* *him.* …*Go.*

conversations.title: Caramon Majere, the Brother Who Stayed

variants.text: *(The big man shifts to make room on the log beside him though you have not asked, an old reflex of welcome, and scrubs again at his eyes with the heel of one enormous hand.)* …Sorry. Sorry, you caught me — *(a wet laugh)* …I do this. Just sort of leak, these days. Used to be I could hold it for the others' sake, keep the big dumb grin on so nobody worried, but down here there's nobody to keep it on *for*, so it just… comes out. *(he looks at the fire)* …Caramon Majere. Warrior, mostly. Cook, when there's anything to cook. Big brother by about thirty heartbeats — *(his face does something complicated)* …that last one's the only job that ever really mattered to me, and it's the one I was no good at, and it's the only one I'd've died to keep. *(he meets your eyes, open as a window)* …I've got a twin, friend. Raistlin. He's — you'll have seen him, maybe, down the other end, with the books and the gold skin and the eyes that look at you like you're already a skeleton. *(a fond, devastated smile)* …He's the cleverest soul that ever lived, my brother. And the strongest, in the way that counts, the inside way — I'm the one with the shoulders but *he's* the one who was never once afraid of anything. …And he spent his whole life pushing me away with both hands. *(quietly)* …And I spent mine letting him think he'd managed it, and never once going. …You want to sit a while, friend? …It's a long story, and a sad one, and I never get to tell it, 'cause the only person I ever wanted to tell it *to* …is the one it's *about.**

nodes.speaker: Caramon Majere
nodes.text: *(He settles his big frame, hands open on his knees, and there is in his whole bearing the relief of a soul that has been alone with something heavy and has just been offered, unexpectedly, a second pair of hands.)* …Ask me anything, friend. *(a crooked smile)* …I'm an easy read — never learned to be anything else. Raish always said I had a face like an open book with the words writ large for the slow. …He meant it unkind, mostly. *(softly)* …I always sort of liked it, though. …Least nobody ever had to *wonder* if I loved 'em.

choices.text: *(read the brother — past the big simple warrior, to the size of what he carries)* "Let me look at you a moment, Caramon. Really look — at all of it, the whole weight you're carrying so easy."
"How do you do it, Caramon? Love someone that hard who never once let you? Most souls would have gone cold. You didn't. Why not?"

*(the one who knows love that keeps no score is the only kind the Wall can't price)* "Caramon — I need you to hear something, because you've had it wrong your whole life and death. You think you failed at the one job that mattered. You think because you couldn't fix him, couldn't make him let you in, couldn't be *needed* the way you needed to be — that your love was wasted. It's the exact opposite, and I can prove it. A love that only counts if it's returned is a *trade* — I give, you give back, we're square. That's not what you did. You loved a man who gave you *nothing* back, who pushed and scorned and shut the door, and you stayed, and you kept loving, and you asked for *nothing* — and that's not a failed transaction, Caramon, because it was never a transaction at all. It's the only kind of love that was ever pure: the kind that keeps no score. He couldn't take it from you because you never put it on his books to begin with. You didn't love him to be loved back. You loved him because he was your brother and that was the whole of the reason. My Realm's Wall prices souls by what they're owed, what comes back, what balances. Your love doesn't even *register* on a wall like that — not because it's worth nothing, but because it's the one thing in all creation that was never *for* sale. You weren't bad at the job, Caramon. You were so good at it that the world doesn't have a number high enough to write it down."
"Knowing how it all went, Caramon — every push, every door shut in your face — would you do it again? Love him the same? Or would you spare yourself?"
"Keep that seat warm, Caramon. I'll come back — and I think I might have something to carry down the hall for you."

nodes.speaker: Caramon Majere
nodes.text: *(He sits still and lets you, the open face turned up to yours, hiding nothing because he has never in his life known how, and you let your sense move past the great shoulders and the easy strength to the weight they were really built to carry.)* *(And the size of his love is the thing that staggers you — not its drama, but its sheer, patient, uncomplaining *mass.* Your sense reads a lifetime of it: a boy of three who already understood, the way some children just do, that his clever twin was sick and small and frightened, and that his own job — assigned by no one, chosen before he had words for choosing — was to be the *wall* the world's cruelty broke against before it could reach his brother. Your sense reads decades of carrying — literally, the frail twin too weak to walk far, hauled on those broad shoulders across half a world; and otherwise, the thousand smaller carryings, the insults absorbed, the meals cooked, the watches stood, the wounds taken, all of it given freely to a brother who repaid it with scorn, with cutting words, with the constant wounding message that Caramon's strength was *stupidity* and his love a *weakness* to be exploited and despised. And your sense reads the thing that breaks your heart clean through: that Caramon *believed* him. That this vast, good, loving man went through his whole life more than half-convinced that his brother was *right* — that he *was* slow, that he *was* a fool, that his love *was* a kind of dumb animal devotion unworthy of the brilliant soul it was aimed at. And he loved him anyway. Not proudly, not as a martyr — your sense finds no martyrdom in him, which is the wonder of it — but simply, helplessly, the way the sun is warm, expecting nothing, keeping no count. Your sense reads that the deepest grief in him is not that Raistlin was cruel. It is smaller and more terrible than that: it is that Caramon would have given *anything* — his strength, his life, the breath in his body — simply to be *needed* by his brother, openly, just once, without the gold armor in the way. And he never was. He spent a lifetime being *indispensable* to a man who would die before admitting he needed *anyone.* Your sense reads, last, the thing he does not even know he is doing: that he is still, here, at the far end of this hall, keeping the seat beside him warm. Still saving a place. Still, after everything, after death itself, *waiting* for his brother to cross the room.)*

nodes.speaker: Caramon Majere
nodes.text: *(He's quiet a while, turning a stick over in his big hands, feeding it slowly to the fire, and when he answers it's without a scrap of self-pity, just a man telling you how a thing simply is.)* …How do I do it. *(a soft breath)* …Folks asked me that, you know, alive. The ones who saw how he treated me. *'Why do you take it, Caramon? Why do you keep going back? He doesn't deserve you.'* *(he shakes his big head, fond and sad)* …And I never had a good answer for 'em, 'cause the question never made *sense* to me. *Deserve.* Like love's a *wage.* Like you tot up what a fella *deserves* and you pay out exactly that much and not a copper more. *(he looks at you)* …That's not — friend, that's not what it *is.* Not the real kind. …Raistlin was *sick* when we were born. Came out second, came out *blue*, almost didn't come out at all, and they put him in my arms — two newborns, but they put *him* in *mine*, I don't know why, maybe just to free up a pair of hands — and I looked at this little *scrap* of a thing, my brother, fighting for every breath… *(his voice thickens)* …and something in me just *decided*, friend. Before I could talk. Before I knew anything. *'That one's mine to look after. Whatever it costs. Forever.'* *(quietly)* …And once you've *decided* a thing like that — *really* decided it, in the place under the thinking — then it doesn't *matter* what he does after. The deciding's already *done.* He could push me away a thousand times — he *did* — and every single time, the part of me that *decided*, when we were *babies*, that he was *mine to keep* …it just — it didn't *get* the message that we were *through.* *(a wet laugh)* …I think some folks are built to love like a *door*, friend — open if you're nice, shut if you're not. …I got built to love like the *weather.* …It don't ask if you *deserve* the rain. …It just *falls* on you, 'cause that's what it *is*, and what *you* do about it was never *its* business. *(softly)* …He never let me in. …But you don't love somebody to get *let in*, friend. …Leastways I never learned how to. …You just love 'em. …And you keep the seat warm. …And you *wait.*

nodes.speaker: Caramon Majere
nodes.text: *(The big man goes very still. The stick stops turning in his hands. And then his open, weathered, unhideable face simply crumples, and he puts his face in those great hands and weeps — not the leaking he apologized for before, but something deeper breaking loose, something that has been dammed up behind forty years of being the one who's all right.)* …*'So good at it the world doesn't have a number high enough.'* *(muffled, wrecked)* …Friend. *Friend.* …Do you know what I — *(he drags his hands down, and his eyes are streaming, and he doesn't try to stop them)* …All this *while.* All this *long* *while* down here, by this *fire.* …I have been so *sure* I *failed* him. *(his voice cracks open)* …That's the — that's the thing that won't let me *be*, friend, the thing I turn over and over. Not that he was *cruel* to me. I never minded that, not really, I had the *shoulders* for it. …It's that I had *one* *job.* The job I gave *myself* when they put him in my arms still *blue.* *Look* after Raistlin. *Whatever* it *costs.* …And I *couldn't.* I couldn't *fix* him, couldn't make him *let* me *in*, couldn't make him *need* me out *loud* even *once* — and so I died thinking I'd *muffed* the *only* thing I was ever *for.* *(he grips your arm, huge hand trembling)* …And you're telling me — you're telling me the job was never *fixing* him. The job was never getting *let* *in.* The job was just — *loving* him, with no *books*, no *score*, no *getting-paid-back* — *(a great shuddering breath)* …and that I *did* that. …That I did that *every* *single* *day* of *both* our *lives* and *never* *once* *stopped.* *(he laughs, and it's half a sob)* …I *did*, friend. …I *did* do that. …That part I *never* failed at. …Not *once.* *(and he scrubs his face, and looks at you with a dawning, terrible, hopeful thing)* …Your *Wall.* The one you're trying to bring down. …You said it can't *price* a love like mine. …Friend — *(barely)* …does that mean — all those *souls* in it, the ones nobody *owed* anything, the ones whose *love* never got *paid* *back* — …does that mean they're not *worth* *less?* …Does that mean they're maybe worth so *much* the wall just *can't* *count* it? *(his voice fills)* …Then you *tell* 'em, friend. …You tell *every* one of 'em what you just told *me.* …That a love that keeps no *score* …isn't a love that *failed.* …It's the only kind that was ever the *real* *thing.* …*(quietly, wrecked, certain)* …And — friend? …When you go back down that hall? …*(he can hardly say it)* …If my brother — if *Raistlin* — …if he ever says my *name* …you tell him the *seat's* still *warm.* …You tell him I *never* *got* the *message* we were *through.* …You tell him I'm *right* *here.* …I've *always* *been* *right* *here.*

nodes.speaker: Caramon Majere
nodes.text: *(He doesn't even have to think about it, and that's the heartbreak of the answer — the immediacy of it, the way it's already decided before the question's done.)* …Would I do it again. *(a soft, certain breath)* …In a *heartbeat*, friend. The *same.* Every *push*, every *cut*, every *door* shut in my *face* — all of it, *again*, exactly the *same.* *(he sees your expression, and smiles, gentle)* …You're thinking that's *foolish.* Most do. *'Spare* yourself, Caramon. *Love* him *less.* He'd *never* know the *difference* — he didn't *want* it *anyway.'* *(he shakes his head)* …But see, that's the part folks never get, friend. *(quietly)* …They think the *love* was the *cost.* The *thing* I *paid.* …It *wasn't.* The love was the thing I *got* to *keep.* *(he meets your eyes, and there's iron under the softness)* …All those years, friend — the world looked at me and saw a big dumb fella getting *used* by his clever brother, *giving* and *giving* and getting *nothing* back, and they *pitied* me for it. …But here's what *none* of 'em understood: …I was the *rich* one. …*He* had the *power*, the *Art*, the *brilliance*, the whole *world* *trembling* at those *eyes* — and inside, friend, he was *cold*, and *alone*, and *terrified*, *every* *single* *day*, *guarding* a thing he could *never* set *down.* …And *me?* …*I* got to *love* somebody with my *whole* *self*, *no* *holding* *back*, *no* *fear* of it, *no* *armor* — *(his voice fills)* …and *that*, friend, the *loving* *freely*, the *not* *being* *afraid* to *give* it *all* *away* — …*that* is the *warmest* a *soul* can *ever* *be.* …He spent his *life* getting *rich* on *power* and *froze.* …I spent *mine* getting *rich* on *loving* him …and I was *warm* the *whole* *way* *through*, friend, *even* when he was *cruel*, *even* at the *end.* …So *no.* …I wouldn't *spare* myself a *thing.* …Why would I give back the *only* *fortune* I ever *had?*

nodes.speaker: Caramon Majere
nodes.text: *(He nods, and gives you that big lopsided grin, but his eyes have caught on the word you used — *carry something down the hall* — and a hope he doesn't dare name has lit somewhere behind them.)* …Yeah. Yeah, go on, friend. *(he wipes his face, gathers himself)* …Go see the others. The *knight*, Sturm — best man I ever stood a line beside, and the *straightest*; if he tells you a thing, it's *true*, he doesn't know how to bend one. *(and then, carefully, so carefully, like a man setting his whole heart on a table and trying to look like he isn't)* …And — *(he swallows)* …and if you go down the *far* end. To the *books.* …To *Raish.* *(his voice goes very quiet)* …I'm not — I'd never *ask* you to *fix* it, friend. People have *tried.* You *can't.* He's the *cleverest* soul that *ever* *lived* and he's *armored* like a *fortress* and a *thousand* people *smarter* than *me* have *bounced* right *off* him. *(barely)* …But you said you might have something to *carry.* …So if — *if* — he should *happen* to say my *name.* …Or *ask* after me. …Or even just — *(his voice breaks)* …even just *look* up the *hall*, the way I *catch* him *doing*, sometimes, when he *thinks* I *don't* *see* — …you don't have to *say* anything *clever*, friend. …You just tell him *one* thing, *plain*, the way *I'd* say it if he'd *ever* *let* me *close* enough to: …*'Your brother kept the seat warm.'* *(he looks at the fire, and the empty place beside him)* …That's *all.* …That's the *whole* *message.* …He'll *know* the *rest.* …He *always* *knew* the *rest.* …That was *never* …that was *never* the *thing* that was *broken* *between* us.*
